goal: Become confident and enjoy weekday cooking; reduce intimidation; establish a sustainable weekly routine (3 weeknight dinners + 1 weekend practice per week).
Establish 3 go-to templates: one-pan roast, stir-fry, simple pasta; and 3 core techniques as a practice framework.
Develop a reliable, tasty weeknight dinner plan you actually want to eat.
constraints: Weeknight dinners should be 20–30 minutes or less.
Build weekly templates: sheet-pan, stir-fry, pasta, grain bowls.
Weeknights: 20–45 minutes.
Weekend: 60–120 minutes.
New vs repeat: aim for 1–2 new recipes per week; repeat or riff on the rest.
Limit experimentation to at most one experiment per week.
Batch or prep components on weekends to shorten weeknights.
Rest days when you feel drained.
Knife practice sessions should be 10–15 minutes, 3–5 times per week.
Stabilize the cutting board with a damp towel or non-slip mat during practice.
Always use the claw grip on your guide hand and keep fingertips tucked.
Use the right knife for the task: chef's knife (8-10 inches) for most tasks; paring knife for small work.
Keep knives sharp and store safely; avoid submerging knives in soapy water during cleaning.
Integrate knife drills into real cooking sessions to transfer the skill.
Oven unavailable; replace baking recipes with stovetop, pressure cooker/Instant Pot, microwave, blender, or other non-baking methods.
Equipment constraints: two-burner gas stove, microwave, rice cooker, blender; no oven.
Weeknights: about 30 minutes on most evenings.
All recipes and pantry items must be free of tree nuts and peanuts.
Avoid cross-contamination with tree nuts, peanuts, coconut, and sesame unless explicitly confirmed safe.
Weekly grocery budget target: around $60.
decisions: Choose 3 go-to templates: one-pan roast, stir-fry, simple pasta.
Choose 3 core techniques: knife basics, seasoning/tasting, heat control & pan finishing.
Aim for 3–5 cooking sessions per week.
Session length targets: Weeknights 20–45 minutes; Weekend 60–120 minutes.
Adopt backward timeline timing method for coordinating multiple components.
First month focus: pan-searing + pan sauces, stir-fries, one-pot pastas, and legume curries/stews.
Secondary month focus: blender sauces, quick pickles/finishers, skillet roasting, and eggs.
Practice rhythm: pick two primary categories for weeknights to repeat and one weekend session to try a new technique/recipe from a secondary category.
entities: pan-seared proteins + quick pan sauces
Stir-fries and high-heat sautés
One-pot pastas and skillet “pasta in a pan”
Braises, stews & pressure-cooker dishes (stovetop or Instant Pot)
Curries, legumes, and thick stews
Skillet “roasting” & char/blister techniques
Eggs: omelettes, shakshuka, stovetop frittata
Blender sauces, pestos, and dressings
Quick pickles, relishes, and crunchy toppings (stovetop or jar)
Microwave & pan-steam hacks for veg and starches
Rice cooker meals (grain bowls, stews, and stove-free bases)
Chinese / Southeast Asian stir-fry style
Indian / South Asian stovetop curries and dals
Middle Eastern / North African stove-cooked dishes
Southeast Asian curries & coconut-based stews
Latin American / Mexican skillet cooking
Japanese / donburi and skillet comfort food
Cross-style techniques: searing and pan-sauces
Stir-fry timing and mise en place
Toasting and tempering spices
Coconut-milk simmering and balancing acid
Quick pickles and charring for contrast
Blender sauces for bowls and dressings
30-minute weeknight meals
Nut-free substitutions for pesto and toppings: use pumpkin seeds or sunflower seeds for crunch or as pesto components.
Yogurt, tahini (if sesame is safe), sunflower-seed butter, or extra olive oil + cooked beans as creamy bases.
Replace nut oils with olive oil, avocado oil, or neutral oils in dressings and finishing.
Garnishes: toasted seeds, toasted oats, or crisped buckwheat (kasha) as nut-free finishes.
Omit crushed peanuts in Asian sauces; substitute extra sesame seeds or crispy fried shallots/garlic if sesame is safe.
Keep a dedicated nut-free prep area and utensils at home; avoid cross-contact and ensure separate oil for frying.
Read labels for 'may contain' warnings; choose products labeled nut-free or produced in nut-free facilities when possible.
open: Confirm which of peanuts, coconut, and sesame are allergens and the severity; determine cross-contamination risk and plan to tailor recipes and pantry lists.
Budget constraints: confirm weekly grocery budget around $60 and number of people to feed; align plan accordingly.
